food_name: Bianco Sicilia DOC White Wine (Passocalcara Musita 2016)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'white wine' class (FOODON_03301699); no specific DOC Sicilia entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LK covers white wine generically; no Sicilia DOC-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 53410008 covers wine as a substance; no white-wine-specific or DOC-specific descendant code confirmed in public browser. Closest parent match used.

main_ingredients: white grapes (Sicilian varieties, likely Catarratto, Grillo, or Inzolia)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of white wine is approximately 150 ml (one glass), equivalent to roughly 150 g; a full standard bottle contains 750 ml (approximately 750 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Wine, white, all table wines (FDC ID 174837)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Bianco Sicilia DOC Passocalcara Musita 2016 is a specific Italian DOC white wine not individually listed in USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FDC entry for generic white table wine (FDC ID 174837), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. Energy value of 82 kcal/100 ml is consistent with a dry-to-off-dry white wine at approximately 11–13% ABV. Input record energy of 80 kcal/100 g is within the expected range and has been updated to 82 kcal to align with the USDA reference.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for Bianco Sicilia DOC white wine. GI of approximately 45 (low category) is estimated by analogy with dry white wine entries referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. White wine has a low GI due to its low residual carbohydrate content (~2.6 g/100 g) and the inhibitory effect of ethanol on gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (45 × 2.6 × 1.5) / 100 = approximately 1.6 g, confirming negligible glycemic impact per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Ethanol content (~11–13% ABV) directly raises blood pressure and interacts with antihypertensive medications
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this alcoholic beverage entirely, as regular alcohol consumption raises systolic blood pressure by 1–2 mmHg per standard drink and antagonises the effect of antihypertensive agents including ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and beta-blockers; the low sodium content (5 mg/100 g) does not offset the pressor effect of ethanol.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Ethanol causes unpredictable blood glucose fluctuations and interacts with diabetes medications including metformin, insulin, and sulfonylureas
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this wine or consume it only occasionally with food under medical supervision, as ethanol inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis and can precipitate hypoglycaemia — particularly dangerous when combined with insulin or sulfonylureas — while sulphites may cause adverse reactions in sensitive individuals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Ethanol has a dual cardiovascular profile: low-to-moderate intake may modestly raise HDL cholesterol, but regular or excess intake increases risk of arrhythmia, cardiomyopathy, and hypertriglyceridaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with established cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid alcohol; while observational data suggest modest wine consumption may be associated with reduced CVD risk, current cardiology guidelines do not recommend initiating alcohol consumption for cardiovascular benefit, and any intake should be discussed with a treating physician.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ethanol is directly pro-inflammatory and interacts with methotrexate and biologic DMARDs, increasing hepatotoxicity risk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate or biologic therapy should avoid this wine entirely, as alcohol potentiates hepatotoxicity of these medications and promotes systemic inflammation that directly worsens joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bianco Sicilia DOC Passocalcara Musita 2016 is a vintage Italian white wine produced in Sicily under the Denominazione di Origine Controllata (DOC) appellation, vinified by the Passocalcara estate under the Musita label. The 2016 vintage presents a pale yellow to golden colour with aromatic notes of acacia blossom, honey, white flowers, and tropical pineapple. On the palate it is full-bodied, persistent, and harmonious with ripe fruit character typical of Sicilian white varieties such as Catarratto, Grillo, or Inzolia. The wine is fermented and aged using traditional winemaking techniques. Per 100 ml it provides approximately 82 kcal, 2.6 g carbohydrate (predominantly residual sugars ~1.4 g), negligible protein (0.07 g), and zero fat. Sodium is low at 5 mg/100 ml. The primary caloric contribution comes from ethanol (~11–13% ABV), which contributes approximately 7 kcal per gram. Clinically, this is an alcoholic beverage and is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or rheumatoid arthritis on disease-modifying therapy. It carries a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease patients. Sulphites are present as a preservative and may cause adverse reactions in sensitive individuals. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low category) with a negligible glycemic load of ~1.6 per standard 150 ml glass, reflecting the low residual carbohydrate content and ethanol-mediated inhibition of glucose absorption.

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central generic white table wine entry (FDC ID 174837), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. The specific Bianco Sicilia DOC Passocalcara Musita 2016 is not individually listed in any approved nutrient database. Values are consistent with the expected profile for a dry-to-off-dry Sicilian white wine at 11–13% ABV. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Protein_g value of 0.07 g is a confirmed trace amount per USDA FoodData Central white wine reference (FDC ID 174837); it is not naturally zero but is nutritionally negligible. Fat_g is naturally zero for white wine — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174837); white wine contains no fat by composition. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, and polyunsaturated_fat_g are confirmed zero per the same USDA reference. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as wine is a liquid fermented beverage with no fibre. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore and region as Southeast Asia, which are incorrect for this product — corrected to Sicily, Italy. The input ethnic_tags included Pasta, Pizza, and Chicken Dish, which are irrelevant to a wine product and have been removed. The input portion of 750 g (1 standard bottle) has been retained as an available portion; a standard 150 ml glass has been set as the default serving for nutritional context. Vegan flag set to false as many wines use animal-derived fining agents (isinglass, casein, egg albumin, gelatin); vegetarian status set to true as a conservative default. Halal flag set to false as this is an alcoholic beverage. Kosher status unverified — set to false. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to 0.0 per USDA FDC 174837 which reports these as zero for white wine. Ontology match types corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language.english and aliases_by_language.romanized appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
